title: Withdrawal from CIRP
content:
Session 7: Withdrawal of resolution plan [¬ß12A I&BC 2016; Regulation 30A and  Regulation 36-B(4-A) of the 
CIRP Regulations 2016]  
Vallal RCK v Siva Industries [2022 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã IDBI Bank (FC) initiated CIRP under S.7 against Siva (CD) ‚Äì 1 applicant submitted a resolution plan ‚Äì RP placed it before 
the CoC ‚Äì received only 60% voting share (failed to meet the statutory requirement of 66%) 
‚ùñ‚Äã RP sought to initiate liquidation with One-Time Settlement (OTS) proposal (to be drawn by IDBI) 
‚û¢‚Äã Not a regular resolution plan under the Code; it was an out-of-CIRP settlement. 
‚û¢‚Äã CoC approved the OTS by 94.23% voting share (i.e., very high, surpassing the 90% threshold required for 
withdrawal under s.12A). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Withdrawal Application: 
‚û¢‚Äã Applicant moved a withdrawal application under Section 12A, Section 60(5) and Rule 30A of the CIRP 
Regulations. 
‚û¢‚Äã RP submitted it to NCLT for approval as required. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Orders of NCLT and NCLAT: 
55 
 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLT rejected the withdrawal application ‚Üí the plan was not a settlement simpliciter under S.12A, but a 
Business Restructuring Plan (i.e., something larger than a simple settlement). 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT upheld the NCLT‚Äôs rejection. 
‚û¢‚Äã Appeal filed before Supreme Court. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Primary Issue: Whether the NCLT and NCLAT can sit in appeal over the CoC‚Äôs decision to approve a withdrawal 
application under Section 12A supported by 90%+ CoC voting share? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Ancillary Issues: 
‚û¢‚Äã Is the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom under Section 12A subject to judicial review? 
‚û¢‚Äã When, if at all, can adjudicating authorities interfere with a Section 12A withdrawal approved by the CoC? 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Statutory Provisions 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 12A, IBC: Permits withdrawal of CIRP after admission of an insolvency application. Requires 90% voting 
share of the CoC for approval. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 60(5): NCLT has jurisdiction over questions of law/fact relating to the CIRP. 
‚û¢‚Äã Rule 30A of CIRP Regulations: Sets out the procedure for withdrawal applications under s.12A. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Case Law and Judicial Principles 
‚û¢‚Äã Swiss Ribbons v. Union of India (2019): 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Constitutionality of s.12A upheld. 
‚óè‚Äã
The 90% threshold is not arbitrary, it belongs to legislative policy. 
‚óè‚Äã
WHY?? ‚Üí withdrawal ordinarily requires an omnibus settlement across the body of financial 
creditors. 
‚óè‚Äã
However, NCLT/NCLAT can interfere if CoC arbitrarily rejects a just settlement. 
‚û¢‚Äã Commercial Wisdom of CoC (various SC cases): 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom is paramount. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
JR is very narrow and limited to: 
‚óè‚Äã
Capriciousness 
‚óè‚Äã
Arbitrariness 
‚óè‚Äã
Irrationality 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Actions de hors (outside) the statute or rules 
‚û¢‚Äã Stringency of Section 12A: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Amendment Act 26 of 2018 reduced CoC threshold for resolution plan approval from 75% to 66%, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
But s.12A retained the 90% requirement, showing the legislature‚Äôs intention of greater stringency for 
withdrawal.. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Nature of the Settlement Plan 
‚û¢‚Äã SC noted that although NCLT called it a ‚ÄúBusiness Restructuring Plan,‚Äù the CoC had considered it as a settlement 
leading to withdrawal. 
‚û¢‚Äã The actual nature of the plan is for CoC to evaluate, not for NCLT to relabel. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CoC‚Äôs Deliberative Process 
‚û¢‚Äã Minutes of the 13th, 14th, 15th CoC meetings showed: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Extensive deliberations. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
CoC members considered every aspect of the settlement. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Suggestions were made; revisions were incorporated by the applicant. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
A dissenting member with 23.60% voting share eventually shifted to support the plan. 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLT itself had directed reconvening of the CoC meeting on 1 April 2021. 
56 
 
‚û¢‚Äã The 17th CoC meeting thereafter approved the settlement with 94.23% votes. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial Overreach by NCLT/NCLAT 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLT and NCLAT essentially substituted their opinion for that of the CoC by: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Recharacterising the settlement as a ‚Äúbusiness restructuring plan.‚Äù 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Treating it as not fitting s.12A despite overwhelming creditor support. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Supreme Court held this was impermissible: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
When 90% or more of the CoC approve, NCLT/NCLAT cannot sit in appeal over the commercial 
wisdom of the CoC. 
‚óè‚Äã
Once the CIRP process begins, it is no longer a proceeding between the applicant creditor and 
the CD, but involves ALL creditors and the Debtor ‚Üí intent is to discourage individual actions 
for enforcement and settlement to the exclusion of the general benefit of all creditors ‚áí 
therefore, 90% requirement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Only Limited Grounds for Interference 
‚û¢‚Äã Interference is justified only if the decision is: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Wholly capricious 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Arbitrary 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Irrational 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
De hors the statute/rules 
‚û¢‚Äã In this case, the CoC decision was well-reasoned, deliberated, revised, and achieved near-unanimous support. 
‚û¢‚Äã Hence none of the grounds existed. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Purpose of Section 12A 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court reiterated the purpose of s.12A: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
To allow efficient exits from CIRP where the creditors collectively determine that settlement is preferable. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The high threshold ensures collective consensus. 
‚û¢‚Äã Judicial bodies must therefore defer to the CoC in such decisions. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Supreme Court held that NCLT and NCLAT had no authority to interfere with a withdrawal application under s.12A 
that had received 94.23% CoC approval. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom under s.12A holds paramountcy, and judicial intervention is permissible only where the CoC‚Äôs 
decision is capricious, arbitrary, irrational, or contrary to the IBC/Regulations, none of which were present here. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court therefore allowed the appeal, upheld the CoC-approved settlement proposal, and held that the withdrawal was 
lawful and should be permitted. 
 
Ebix Singapore Private Limited v Committee of Creditors [2021 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Educomp Solutions Ltd. (CD) ‚Äì voluntary application under Section 10, admitted by NCLT ‚Üí Successful Resolution 
Applicant (SRA) is Ebix Singapore, submitted a plan which was approved by the CoC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã BUT before Section 31 approval by NCLT, Ebix sought: 
‚û¢‚Äã Withdrawal of the approved plan; or 
‚û¢‚Äã Modification of certain terms 
‚ùñ‚Äã Reasons cited by Ebix: 
‚û¢‚Äã Multiple investigations pending against CD ‚Äì CBI, SFIO. 
‚û¢‚Äã COVID-19 affected the financial capacity of SRA. 
‚û¢‚Äã Delays ‚Äì CoC approval pending 17 months; CIRP ongoing 26 months. 
‚û¢‚Äã Fear of erosion of the value of the business (government contracts expiring; media reports about wrongdoing). 
‚û¢‚Äã The academic calendar requires urgent takeover ‚Üí time is of the essence. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLT allowed withdrawal, but the order was challenged. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLAT reversed it (held RP is contractual & estoppel applies). 
57 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue reaches SC. 
 
Issue: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Does Section 12A apply to successful resolution applicants (SRAs)? ‚Üí Can SRAs use s.12A as an exit route after CoC 
approval? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Can an SRA withdraw or modify a resolution plan after CoC approval but before NCLT approval under Section 31? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is a CoC-approved resolution plan (pending NCLT approval) a binding contract? 
‚û¢‚Äã Is it merely an offer that can be revoked? 
‚û¢‚Äã Is it a contingent contract subject to Section 31? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 12A ‚Äì Only withdrawal of CIRP applications under Sections 7, 9, 10. 
‚û¢‚Äã SC in Maharashtra Seamless ‚Üí exit route NOT available to SRAs. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 40A & 40B ‚Äì strict model timelines; deviations punishable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulations 37-38 ‚Äì mandatory contents of Resolution Plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 31 ‚Äì plan binding on all stakeholders; violation = penalty under Section 74, not damages. 
‚ùñ‚Äã SC rejects use of Section 60(5)(c) (residuary jurisdiction) to permit SRA withdrawal ‚Üí Would destroy timelines; contrary to 
Essar Steel & Gujarat Urja. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Casus omissus ‚Äì courts cannot read in an exit route when the legislature omitted it deliberately. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No equivalent to Section 18(3)(b) of SICA ‚Üí IBC intentionally excludes post-approval modification powers. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Resolution process has three broad stages: 
‚û¢‚Äã before and up to the CoC‚Äôs approval of the Resolution Plan; 
‚û¢‚Äã the interim period between CoC approval and the Adjudicating Authority‚Äôs confirmation; and 
‚û¢‚Äã after the Adjudicating Authority approves the plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã In the first stage, party rights and obligations are clearly governed by the IBC, for example when a prospective resolution 
applicant seeks the IM or RFRP after submitting an EOI, even if the RP has rejected it. In the third stage, Section 31(1) 
makes the approved Resolution Plan binding on all stakeholders, with violations attracting penalties under Section 74. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The focus here is the second, interim stage. To understand the parties‚Äô relationship during this period, it is necessary to 
examine the legal character of a Resolution Plan once it is submitted to the AA but before approval under Section 31(1). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is RP a contract? ‚Üí NO 
‚û¢‚Äã SC gives multiple reasons: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Lack of privity: 
‚óè‚Äã
RP binds all stakeholders, even dissenting creditors. 
‚óè‚Äã
No contract can bind non-consenting parties ‚Üí this is a statutory creation. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Statutory governance of every step: 
‚óè‚Äã
Timelines, voting thresholds (66%), contents, treatment of dissenting creditors, waterfall under 
s.53 ‚Äì all fixed. 
‚óè‚Äã
These are NOT bargaining outcomes between free contracting parties. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
IBC = Self-contained Code: 
‚óè‚Äã
Injecting contract law principles will cause chaos & protracted litigation. 
‚óè‚Äã
Remedy for breach is NOT damages (contract) 
‚óè‚Äã
Its penalty under Section 74 ‚Üí statutory consequences ‚Üí confirms non-contractual nature. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Even pre-Section 31 stage: 
‚óè‚Äã
Plan is binding inter se CoC & SRA. 
‚óè‚Äã
‚ÄúStatute confers enforceability; not private consent.‚Äù 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
BLRC Report notes RP is binding even before Section 31 approval. 
58 
 
‚û¢‚Äã SC rejects arguments that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
RP = ‚Äúoffer‚Äù until NCLT approval 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Or RP = contingent contract 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
‚Üí Statutory process overrides common law principles. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 12A does NOT apply to SRAs 
‚û¢‚Äã The section applies only to applicants who initiated CIRP ‚Äì BUT ‚Äì SRA is not the applicant under Section 
7/9/10. 
‚û¢‚Äã Allowing S12A withdrawal would: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
distort timelines 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
defeat IBC‚Äôs purpose (timely resolution & value max.) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
create uncertainty in CIRP 
‚ùñ‚Äã Can an SRA withdraw or modify after CoC approval? ‚Üí NO 
‚û¢‚Äã Allowing the AA to use S.60(5) to permit withdrawal or modification of a CoC-approved RP contradicts Essar 
Steel, which held that s.60(5)(c) cannot dilute S.31(1) or introduce equitable powers beyond S.30(2).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Recognising such a power would disrupt timelines, delay CIRP, erode value, and undermine certainty.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
If such withdrawals are to be allowed, it is for Parliament to decide. 
‚û¢‚Äã On the statute‚Äôs terms, the AA has no power to permit a successful Resolution Applicant to withdraw or modify a 
plan or enforce withdrawal clauses.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Unlike SICA, S.31 only permits approval or rejection; rejection leads to mandatory liquidation under 
S.33.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
S.60(5)(c) cannot be used to create a power the IBC intentionally omits, as reaffirmed in Gujarat Urja. 
‚û¢‚Äã Given the 330-day limit under S.12, withdrawals or renegotiation would undermine timely resolution.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Parliament has created no mechanism to restart timelines post-submission, even though it has elsewhere, 
such as Regulation 36B(5). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Ebix‚Äôs withdrawal arguments fail.  
‚û¢‚Äã The RFRP, the six-month validity clause, and frustration due to investigations apply only during negotiation with 
the CoC, not after submission to the AA. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The plan remained valid through negotiations until submission on 7 March 2018. 
‚û¢‚Äã Even if the plan was a conditional six-month offer, the LOI, Form H, and RFRP show withdrawal is allowed only 
upon rejection.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Clause 9.1 permits withdrawal if the NCLT rejects the plan; Clause 9.7 allows amendments only for 
implementation and with 75 percent CoC approval.  
‚óè‚Äã
There is no clause allowing withdrawal after CoC approval but before judicial confirmation. 
‚û¢‚Äã Investigations into Educomp are irrelevant.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 32A protects the CD from liability for pre-CIRP offences, removing prosecutorial risk.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Ebix was given all information under S.29; there is no evidence the RP concealed SFIO/CBI inquiries, 
which arose only after plan submission. 
‚û¢‚Äã In sum, neither the IBC nor the plan documents give a Resolution Applicant any right to withdraw or modify a 
plan after CoC approval and submission. Courts cannot create such a right. 
 
Conclusion: The IBC intentionally creates a binding statutory process; a CoC-approved resolution plan is NOT governed by 
contract law and cannot be withdrawn or modified by the SRA after approval by the CoC. 
 
State Bank of India v The Consortium [2024 | SC | 3J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Jet Airways (India) Ltd. (CD) ‚Äì CIRP initiated ‚Äì FC ‚âà ‚Çπ7,800 crore. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Successful Resolution Applicant (SRA): Consortium of natural persons known as the ‚ÄúConsortium of Mr. Murari Lal‚Äù. 
‚û¢‚Äã CoC approved a Resolution Plan submitted by the Consortium. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, post approval, SRA failed to implement the plan in time. 
59 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Stage-4 type scenario: Court considers whether to proceed to liquidation since the plan is not being implemented. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Central dispute: Non-implementation of the approved Resolution Plan by the SRA, despite passage of considerable time, 
rising costs, and depreciation of the CD‚Äôs assets. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Procedural history: Matter reached the Supreme Court where SBI (FC) sought directions regarding the consequences of 
non-implementation. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether non-implementation of an approved Resolution Plan by the SRA should necessarily result in liquidation under 
Section 33(3) of the IBC? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether SC can direct liquidation through Article 142 instead of Section 33. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether NCLT/NCLAT‚Äôs power to extend timelines should be exercised cautiously. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Objectives of the IBC (Preamble) ‚Äì IBC aims at: 
‚û¢‚Äã time-bound resolution, 
‚û¢‚Äã maximisation of asset value, 
‚û¢‚Äã balancing interests of stakeholders, 
‚û¢‚Äã preserving CD as a going concern‚Äîbut not at the cost of efficiency. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 33(3) ‚Äì Liquidation can be ordered if the Resolution Plan is contravened. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 38 & Section 30(2)(d) ‚Äì Every plan must contain: 
‚û¢‚Äã Implementation schedule. 
‚û¢‚Äã Manner of supervision. 
‚û¢‚Äã RP must ensure feasibility, viability, and implementability. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 74(3) ‚Äì Willful and knowing contravention of the approved plan is punishable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Rule 15 (NCLT/NCLAT Rules, 2016) 
‚û¢‚Äã Tribunal can extend timelines only with application of mind. 
‚û¢‚Äã Extension must be justified, carefully weighed. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Article 142 ‚Äì SC can pass orders to ensure complete justice. 
‚û¢‚Äã BUT: Must be consistent with the IBC framework (Ebix; Glas Trust v. Byju). Deviation only in exceptional or 
extraordinary circumstances. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Scope and Limits of the CoC‚Äôs Commercial Wisdom 
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT re-affirmed the centrality of the CoC‚Äôs ‚Äúcommercial wisdom,‚Äù but importantly clarified that such 
wisdom is not absolute. 
‚û¢‚Äã While K. Sashidhar v. Indian Overseas Bank and Essar Steel v. Satish Kumar Gupta (SC) came later, 
NCLAT in Binani laid the groundwork: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
CoC decisions cannot violate the IBC‚Äôs foundational principles, especially: 
‚óè‚Äã
Maximisation of value of assets (s. 31 read with Preamble) 
‚óè‚Äã
Promotion of entrepreneurship 
‚óè‚Äã
Balancing interests of all stakeholders (s. 30(2)(e) and Regulation 38) 
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, a plan approved by CoC can still be judicially interfered with if it is discriminatory or contrary to the 
IBC scheme. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The NCLAT held that CoC‚Äôs business decision must be exercised within the ‚Äúfour corners of the Code.‚Äù  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Commercial wisdom does not mean freedom to approve a resolution plan that: 
‚óè‚Äã
treats similar creditors unequally without rationale, 
‚óè‚Äã
extinguishes claims arbitrarily, 
‚óè‚Äã
or knowingly leads to liquidation value outcomes when a better offer exists. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Discrimination Between Similarly Situated Creditors 
60 
 
‚û¢‚Äã A core issue was that the RPPL plan grossly favoured certain secured creditors (primarily those backing RPPL‚Äôs 
bid) over others. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Differential treatment between: 
‚óè‚Äã
Secured creditors of the same class 
‚óè‚Äã
Operational creditors 
‚óè‚Äã
Dissenting financial creditors 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT held that IBC does not permit discrimination within a class, even though different classes of creditors may 
be treated differently. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The principle relied on: 
‚óè‚Äã
Parity within the same class: All similarly placed FCs must get equitable (not necessarily 
identical) treatment. 
‚óè‚Äã
Fair and equitable treatment under Regulation 38(1A) (as it then stood) required that OC dues 
should not be arbitrarily reduced when FCs are being paid substantially more. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The tribunal emphasised that ‚Äúresolution is not a recovery mechanism‚Äù for one creditor or a group of 
creditors at the expense of others. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Maximisation of Value as a Mandatory Consideration 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT held that the RPPL plan did not maximise the value of the Corporate Debtor because a higher, more 
beneficial offer from Ultratech was never meaningfully considered by the RP/RsP/CoC due to procedural and 
timing issues. 
‚û¢‚Äã The tribunal stressed that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
value maximisation is not merely about ‚Äúhighest bid‚Äù but ensuring the best possible outcome for all 
stakeholders, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
CoC cannot adopt a ‚Äúclosed-door, narrow, or biased‚Äù approach to evaluation, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the IBC is not an ‚Äúauction,‚Äù but competitive and transparent bidding improves value realisation, and 
CoC cannot block a better value-enhancing plan through artificial deadlines or arbitrary insistence on 
earlier submissions. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CoC‚Äôs conduct of not considering the revised Ultratech bid was held malafide or at least influenced by 
extraneous considerations. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Principle that Resolution Should Keep Corporate Debtor as a Going Concern 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT underscored that the objective of a resolution plan is revival, not sale of assets or recovery by creditors. 
‚û¢‚Äã The tribunal pointed out that RPPL‚Äôs plan could have affected the long-term viability of the corporate debtor due 
to: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
insufficient working capital infusion, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
questionable provisioning for statutory dues and operational creditors, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
potential future litigation due to discriminatory treatment‚Äîwhich would frustrate revival. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Role of the Resolution Professional (RP) 
‚û¢‚Äã The RP was criticised for: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
not placing all plans objectively before CoC, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
favouring one resolution applicant (RPPL), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
failing to ensure transparent process, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
failing to ensure compliance of each plan with s. 30(2) and Regulations 37‚Äì38. 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT emphasised that the RP is not merely a ‚Äúpost office‚Äù but a quasi-fiduciary officer with duties to: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
ensure equality, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
ensure proper evaluation, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
avoid bias, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
and place all compliant plans before CoC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Substance Over Form: A Resolution Plan Must Achieve IBC‚Äôs Objectives 
‚û¢‚Äã Even if the CoC approved the RPPL plan, that cannot protect a plan that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
undermines fairness, 
61 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
creates a windfall for certain creditors, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
defeats the purpose of resolution, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
or artificially depresses payouts to operational/dissenting creditors. 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT asserted that the Code cannot be used to create private bargains between subsets of creditors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Transparent and Non-Discriminatory Bidding Process 
‚û¢‚Äã The tribunal held that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Ultratech‚Äôs revised bid was not ‚Äúlate,‚Äù 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
CoC must consider any plan till approval under s. 30(4), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
procedural cut-offs cannot supersede substantive goals of value maximisation. 
‚û¢‚Äã Importantly, NCLAT rejected the idea that once a preferred bidder is identified, the process is closed‚Äîthis would 
distort competition and public interest. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Outcome (Reasoning Leading to Ultratech‚Äôs Plan Approval) ‚Äì 
‚û¢‚Äã Because Ultratech‚Äôs plan: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
offered significantly higher realisations, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
equitably treated all creditors, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
ensured working capital for revival, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Avoided discrimination, 
‚û¢‚Äã NCLAT exercised its appellate jurisdiction and directed that Ultratech‚Äôs plan be accepted.
cases: Swiss Ribbons v. Union of India
Ebix Singapore Private Limited v Committee of Creditors
State Bank of India v The Consortium
Indian Overseas Bank and Essar Steel v. Satish Kumar Gupta
key_principles: ‚Äã IDBI Bank (FC) initiated CIRP under S.7 against Siva (CD) ‚Äì 1 applicant submitted a resolution plan ‚Äì RP placed it before
‚Äã RP sought to initiate liquidation with One-Time Settlement (OTS) proposal (to be drawn by IDBI)
‚Äã Not a regular resolution plan under the Code; it was an out-of-CIRP settlement.
‚Äã CoC approved the OTS by 94.23% voting share (i.e., very high, surpassing the 90% threshold required for
‚Äã Withdrawal Application:
‚Äã Applicant moved a withdrawal application under Section 12A, Section 60(5) and Rule 30A of the CIRP
‚Äã RP submitted it to NCLT for approval as required.
‚Äã Orders of NCLT and NCLAT:
‚Äã NCLT rejected the withdrawal application ‚Üí the plan was not a settlement simpliciter under S.12A, but a
‚Äã NCLAT upheld the NCLT‚Äôs rejection.
‚Äã Appeal filed before Supreme Court.
‚Äã Primary Issue: Whether the NCLT and NCLAT can sit in appeal over the CoC‚Äôs decision to approve a withdrawal
‚Äã Is the CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom under Section 12A subject to judicial review?
‚Äã When, if at all, can adjudicating authorities interfere with a Section 12A withdrawal approved by the CoC?
‚Äã Statutory Provisions
‚Äã Section 12A, IBC: Permits withdrawal of CIRP after admission of an insolvency application. Requires 90% voting
‚Äã Section 60(5): NCLT has jurisdiction over questions of law/fact relating to the CIRP.
‚Äã Rule 30A of CIRP Regulations: Sets out the procedure for withdrawal applications under s.12A.
‚Äã Case Law and Judicial Principles
‚Äã Swiss Ribbons v. Union of India (2019):
